title: Zone or overseas forces
description: How to determine your entitlement to a zone or overseas forces tax offset in myTax.

content_blocks.text: You may be entitled to claim for living in a zone or serving in an overseas force if you:

items: lived or worked in a remote or isolated area of Australia, not including an offshore oil or gas rig, or
served overseas as a member of the Australian Defence Force or a United Nations armed force.

content_blocks.text: If you qualify for both a zone tax offset and an overseas forces tax offset, you can claim only one of them. Claim the one that gives you the higher tax offset.
You may also be eligible to claim additional dependant tax offsets at this item for:

items: your or your spouse's invalid child, brother or sister

content_blocks.text: Zone tax offset
To qualify for the tax offset, you must have lived or worked in a remote area (not necessarily continuously) for 183 days or more during:

items: the period 1 July 2012 to 30 June 2014 (including at least one day in 2013–14), and you did not claim a zone tax offset in your 2013 tax return.

content_blocks.text: If you lived in a zone for less than 183 days in 2013–14, you may still be able to claim a tax offset if all the following conditions apply:

items: you lived in a zone for a continuous period of less than five years after 1 July 2008
you were unable to claim in the first year because you were there less than 183 days, and
the total days you were there in the first year and in 2013–14 is 183 or more.

content_blocks.text: Remote areas are classed as either zone A or zone B. There are also special areas within these zones. If you do not know which zone your area is in, see table 2 below. If your area is not listed, go to Australian zone list to check whether it is in a zone.
Overseas forces tax offset

You may be eligible for an overseas forces tax offset if you served in a specified overseas locality as a member of the Australian Defence Force or a United Nations armed force in 2013–14, and your income relating to that service was not specifically exempt from tax. Periods of service for which your income was ‘exempt foreign employment income’ are excluded in working out your eligibility for the tax offset.
Your employer will be able to advise you or you can check whether you served in a locality that qualifies for the overseas forces tax offset .
If your overseas service was less than 183 days, you may be able to claim part of the tax offset.
To claim the full overseas forces tax offset:

items: you must have served in the overseas locality for 183 days or more in 2013–14, or
the total number of days served in the overseas locality, when added to the number of days spent in one or more zones, must be 183 days or more.

content_blocks.text: If you served as a member of the Australian Defence Force, days spent in a zone must be defence force service.
Completing your tax return
1. Your tax offset is the amount in table 1 below if all the following apply:

items: your circumstances are shown in table 1 below
you lived or worked in only one zone or served in only one specified overseas locality for at least 183 days
you were single, and have no other dependants or housekeeper to claim for, and

you either did not have a dependent child or student at any time in 2013-14, or if you did have a dependent child or student, their adjusted taxable income (ATI) was equal to or greater than $282 plus $28.92 for each week you maintained them.

rows: You must use the Zone or overseas forces tax offset calculator at to work out your tax offset, and then go to step 3.

content_blocks.text: 2. If you are claiming an overseas forces tax offset, go to step 3.

If you are claiming a zone tax offset and you received a remote area allowance from Centrelink or the Department of Veterans’ Affairs, or an equivalent amount was included in an exceptional circumstances relief payment, take this amount away from your zone tax offset.
3. Enter the amount of your tax offset.
* Locations within 250 kilometres of these localities are also in the relevant zone.
* Locations within 250 kilometres of these localities are also in the relevant zone.
* Locations within 250 kilometres of these localities are also in the relevant zone.
If your area is not listed, go to Australian zone list to check whether it is in a zone.

content_text:
You may be entitled to claim for living in a zone or serving in an overseas force if you:
- lived or worked in a remote or isolated area of Australia, not including an offshore oil or gas rig, or
- served overseas as a member of the Australian Defence Force or a United Nations armed force.
If you qualify for both a zone tax offset and an overseas forces tax offset, you can claim only one of them. Claim the one that gives you the higher tax offset.
You may also be eligible to claim additional dependant tax offsets at this item for:
- your or your spouse's parent
- your or your spouse's invalid child, brother or sister
- your child-housekeeper
- your housekeeper.
## Zone tax offset
To qualify for the tax offset, you must have lived or worked in a remote area (not necessarily continuously) for 183 days or more during:
- 2013–14, or
- the period 1 July 2012 to 30 June 2014 (including at least one day in 2013–14), and you did not claim a zone tax offset in your 2013 tax return.
If you lived in a zone for less than 183 days in 2013–14, you may still be able to claim a tax offset if all the following conditions apply:
- you lived in a zone for a continuous period of less than five years after 1 July 2008
- you were unable to claim in the first year because you were there less than 183 days, and
- the total days you were there in the first year and in 2013–14 is 183 or more.
Remote areas are classed as either zone A or zone B. There are also special areas within these zones. If you do not know which zone your area is in, see table 2 below. If your area is not listed, go to Australian zone list to check whether it is in a zone.
## Overseas forces tax offset
You may be eligible for an overseas forces tax offset if you served in a specified overseas locality as a member of the Australian Defence Force or a United Nations armed force in 2013–14, and your income relating to that service was not specifically exempt from tax. Periods of service for which your income was ‘exempt foreign employment income’ are excluded in working out your eligibility for the tax offset.
Your employer will be able to advise you or you can check whether you served in a locality that qualifies for the overseas forces tax offset .
If your overseas service was less than 183 days, you may be able to claim part of the tax offset.
To claim the full overseas forces tax offset:
- you must have served in the overseas locality for 183 days or more in 2013–14, or
- the total number of days served in the overseas locality, when added to the number of days spent in one or more zones, must be 183 days or more.
If you served as a member of the Australian Defence Force, days spent in a zone must be defence force service.
## Completing your tax return
1. Your tax offset is the amount in table 1 below if all the following apply:
- your circumstances are shown in table 1 below
- you lived or worked in only one zone or served in only one specified overseas locality for at least 183 days
- you were single, and have no other dependants or housekeeper to claim for, and
- you either did not have a dependent child or student at any time in 2013-14, or if you did have a dependent child or student, their adjusted taxable income (ATI) was equal to or greater than $282 plus $28.92 for each week you maintained them.
Your circumstances | Zone A | Zone B | Special area | Overseas forces
You were single | $338 | $57 | $1,173 | $338
No | You must use the Zone or overseas forces tax offset calculator at to work out your tax offset, and then go to step 3.
Yes | Read on.
2. If you are claiming an overseas forces tax offset, go to step 3.
If you are claiming a zone tax offset and you received a remote area allowance from Centrelink or the Department of Veterans’ Affairs, or an equivalent amount was included in an exceptional circumstances relief payment, take this amount away from your zone tax offset.
3. Enter the amount of your tax offset.
